searchIndex.title: Installation
searchIndex.description: Pyxle requires:
searchIndex.searchText: Installation Prerequisites Pyxle requires: Python 3.10+ (3.12 recommended) Node.js 18+ (for Vite, React, and SSR) npm (ships with Node.js) Verify your setup: Install Pyxle Install Pyxle from PyPI: This installs the CLI and the framework runtime. Confirm it works: Installing in a virtual environment (recommended) What gets installed The package includes: Component Purpose CLI Project scaffolding, dev server, build pipeline and decorators for your files Configuration loading and validation Starlet

searchIndex.title: Quick Start
searchIndex.description: Create a working Pyxle app in under 5 minutes.
searchIndex.searchText: Quick Start Create a working Pyxle app in under 5 minutes. 1. Scaffold a new project This creates a directory with a complete starter project. 2. Install dependencies This runs both and . You can also run them separately: 3. Start the dev server Open http://localhost:8000 in your browser. You should see the Pyxle starter page — a centered card showing the framework version, server time, and a link to edit . Tailwind compiles automatically because the scaffold ships with — PostCSS runs as part of

See Routing for the full rules. Static files served directly. Anything in is available at the root URL: (generated at runtime) Created automatically when you run or . Contains compiled Python modules, transpiled JSX, and Vite configuration. This directory is gitignored do not edit files here. Key fil

searchIndex.title: .pyxl Files
searchIndex.description: A `.pyxl` file is the fundamental building block of a Pyxle application. It combines Python server logic with a React component in a single file -- your data fetching and your UI live together.
searchIndex.searchText: Files A file is the fundamental building block of a Pyxle application. It combines Python server logic with a React component in a single file your data fetching and your UI live together. Anatomy of a file A file has two sections: The compiler automatically detects which lines are Python and which are JSX. Python code uses / decorators, imports, and standard Python syntax. Everything else is treated as JSX. The Python section The Python section runs entirely on the server. It can: Import module

searchIndex.title: Routing
searchIndex.description: Pyxle uses **file-based routing**. The file structure inside your `pages/` directory determines the URL routes of your application. No router configuration is needed.
searchIndex.searchText: Routing Pyxle uses filebased routing. The file structure inside your directory determines the URL routes of your application. No router configuration is needed. Basic routes Every file in becomes a route: File URL The filename is special it maps to the parent directory's path. Dynamic segments Wrap a filename segment in square brackets to create a dynamic route parameter: File URL Pattern Example Access dynamic parameters in your loader via : Parameter name rules Hyphens in parameter names becom

searchIndex.title: Data Loading
searchIndex.description: Pyxle uses `@server` decorated functions to load data on the server before rendering a page. The loader runs on every request, fetches whatever the page needs, and passes the result as props to the Re
searchIndex.searchText: Data Loading Pyxle uses decorated functions to load data on the server before rendering a page. The loader runs on every request, fetches whatever the page needs, and passes the result as props to the React component. Basic loader The function: 1. Receives a Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/requests/) object 2. Must be (enforced at compile time) 3. Must return a JSONserializable 4. The return value is available as in the React component Accessing request data The parameter is a full Starlet

searchIndex.title: Server Actions
searchIndex.description: Server actions let your React components call Python functions on the server. They are the mutation counterpart to `@server` loaders -- loaders read data, actions write data.
searchIndex.searchText: Server Actions Server actions let your React components call Python functions on the server. They are the mutation counterpart to loaders loaders read data, actions write data. Defining an action Use the decorator on an async function in your file: The function: Must be Receives a Starlette object Must return a JSONserializable Can only be called via request Multiple actions can exist in the same file alongside a loader. Calling actions from React Using the component The simplest way to call an
headings: Invalidating client caches after a mutation

searchIndex.title: Layouts
searchIndex.description: Layouts wrap pages in shared UI -- navigation bars, sidebars, footers, and other elements that persist across page navigations.
searchIndex.searchText: Layouts Layouts wrap pages in shared UI navigation bars, sidebars, footers, and other elements that persist across page navigations. Creating a layout Add a file to any directory inside . It wraps all pages in that directory and its subdirectories: A layout is a React component that receives : Slots Layouts can export a object and a function for passing additional content from pages: Nesting layouts Layouts nest automatically. If both and exist, a page at is wrapped by both: Inner layouts are re

searchIndex.title: Styling
searchIndex.description: Pyxle ships with Tailwind CSS out of the box. The recommended way to load your stylesheets is to **import them from a JSX module** so Vite can compile, bundle, and content-hash them — exactly like it
searchIndex.searchText: Styling Pyxle ships with Tailwind CSS out of the box. The recommended way to load your stylesheets is to import them from a JSX module so Vite can compile, bundle, and contenthash them — exactly like it does for JavaScript modules. This means you never have to handbump a query string after a deploy to invalidate stale browser caches. Recommended: Vitemanaged CSS (autohashed) When your project has a file alongside the Tailwind config, Pyxle delegates CSS processing to Vite + PostCSS. The standalo
headings: Importing CSS from any JSX module

searchIndex.title: Head Management
searchIndex.description: Pyxle offers two ways to control the document `<head>`: the `<Head>` component (**recommended**) and the `HEAD` Python variable. Both merge together with automatic deduplication, and either works — bu
searchIndex.searchText: Head Management Pyxle offers two ways to control the document : the component (recommended) and the Python variable. Both merge together with automatic deduplication, and either works — but we recommend for almost every real page. TL;DR — use the component That's the pattern you'll use for the vast majority of pages. It reads like React, interpolates props naturally, and lives next to the body markup that depends on the same data. Why is the recommended approach It's just JSX. If you know React,
headings: When to prefer the HEAD variable

searchIndex.title: API Routes
searchIndex.description: Files under `pages/api/` are API endpoints. They are plain Python files (not `.pyxl`) that handle HTTP requests and return JSON or other responses.
searchIndex.searchText: API Routes Files under are API endpoints. They are plain Python files (not ) that handle HTTP requests and return JSON or other responses. Basic API route Create : This responds to : HTTP methods Define functions named after HTTP methods: Supported methods: , , , , , . Requests to unsupported methods return . Using HTTPEndpoint classes For more structure, use Starlette's : WebSocket endpoints Since 0.3.0, an API module can export to register a WebSocket handler at the same path. The file can exp

searchIndex.title: Middleware
searchIndex.description: Pyxle supports two levels of middleware: **application-level middleware** that wraps every request, and **route-level hooks** that run only for specific route types.
searchIndex.searchText: Middleware Pyxle supports two levels of middleware: applicationlevel middleware that wraps every request, and routelevel hooks that run only for specific route types. Applicationlevel middleware Add Starlettecompatible middleware classes to your config: Each entry is a string in format. The class must be a standard Starlette middleware or subclass. Writing a middleware Middleware is applied in the order listed in config. The first middleware in the list is the outermost wrapper. Routelevel hooks

searchIndex.title: Environment Variables
searchIndex.description: Pyxle loads environment variables from `.env` files and supports special prefixes for configuration overrides and client-side injection.
searchIndex.searchText: Environment Variables Pyxle loads environment variables from files and supports special prefixes for configuration overrides and clientside injection. files Create files in your project root: Load order Files are loaded in order of increasing precedence. Later files override earlier ones: 1. base defaults 2. or modespecific 3. local overrides (gitignore this) 4. or local mode overrides The mode is when running and when running or . The shell always wins Variables already set in your shell enviro

searchIndex.title: Error Handling
searchIndex.description: Pyxle provides structured error handling through error exceptions, error boundaries, and not-found pages.
searchIndex.searchText: Error Handling Pyxle provides structured error handling through error exceptions, error boundaries, and notfound pages. LoaderError Raise from a function to trigger the nearest error boundary: LoaderError parameters Parameter Type Default Description (required) Error message shown in the error boundary HTTP status code for the response Additional context passed to the error boundary ActionError Raise from an function to return a structured error to the client: The client receives . ActionError p

searchIndex.title: Client Components
searchIndex.description: Pyxle provides built-in React components and hooks importable from `pyxle/client`.
searchIndex.searchText: Client Components Pyxle provides builtin React components and hooks importable from . Manages document elements. See Head Management for full details. Renders nothing in the DOM. During SSR, its children are extracted and merged into the document head. Loads external scripts with configurable loading strategies: Props Prop Type Default Description (required) Script URL Loading strategy Add attribute Add attribute Called when script loads Called on load failure Loading strategies Strategy When it

searchIndex.title: Security
searchIndex.description: Pyxle includes CSRF protection, CORS support, and HEAD element sanitisation out of the box.
searchIndex.searchText: Security Pyxle includes CSRF protection, CORS support, and HEAD element sanitisation out of the box. CSRF protection CSRF (CrossSite Request Forgery) protection is enabled by default using a doublesubmit cookie pattern. How it works 1. Pyxle sets a cookie on every response 2. Statechanging requests (, , , ) must include the cookie value in the header 3. The framework validates that the header matches the cookie using constanttime comparison 4. If they do not match, a response is returned The com
headings: Token validation for custom fetch calls

searchIndex.title: Deployment
searchIndex.description: Deploy a Pyxle application with `pyxle build` to compile assets and `pyxle serve` to run in production.
searchIndex.searchText: Deployment Deploy a Pyxle application with to compile assets and to run in production. Build for production This: 1. Compiles all files into Python and JSX modules 2. Runs — Vite bundles JS and processes CSS through PostCSS (Tailwind is compiled inpipeline when is present, which is the scaffold default) 3. Outputs production artifacts to the directory Build options Serve in production This starts a production Starlette server without Vite (static assets are served directly): Serve options Flag D

searchIndex.searchText: Editor setup Pyxle Language Tools brings firstclass IDE support to files — syntax highlighting, diagnostics, completions, hover documentation, gotodefinition, and formatting. Everything works out of the box with a single install. Beta release. The language toolkit is productionusable but still evolving. Report issues on GitHub. VS Code (recommended) 1. Install the extension Search for "Pyxle" in the VS Code Extensions panel, or install from the terminal: The extension is also available on the VS

searchIndex.title: Pyxle for AI coding agents
searchIndex.description: Copilot, whatever you use — can read, write, debug, and ship real full-stack features with a tiny fraction of the context, files, tools, and languages that traditional frameworks demand. One file per
searchIndex.searchText: Pyxle for AI coding agents TL;DR. Pyxle is built so a coding agent — Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, whatever you use — can read, write, debug, and ship real fullstack features with a tiny fraction of the context, files, tools, and languages that traditional frameworks demand. One file per page, Python backend, React frontend, zero config, structured errors, no magic. If your workflow involves pairing with an AI agent, Pyxle is the framework most optimised for that pairing. This isn't a marketing
headings: The problem agents have with modern web frameworks
One page, one file, both languages
Why this matters for an AI agent specifically
1. Python is the language LLMs know best
2. Python is the language the AI ecosystem lives in
3. No magic — the code you see is the code that runs
4. Conventional file layout is the routing table
5. pyxle check gives agents structured, per-file errors
7. Async by default, enforced at compile time
8. The dev overlay shows agent-friendly errors in real time
10. No codegen, no build artifacts the agent has to maintain
A concrete comparison: shipping a feature
Beyond individual features: what agents really want
The dev server reloads everything the right way
Error messages are written for humans *and* machines
The framework repo itself has a CLAUDE.md
Who should use Pyxle as their agent-first framework?
Python engineers shipping web apps with AI agents
Indie hackers using AI as their engineering team
Getting your agent set up with Pyxle
1. Drop a project-level CLAUDE.md (or equivalent) in your repo
2. Give the agent access to pyxle check and the dev server logs
3. Point the agent at the architecture handbook for deep questions

searchIndex.title: Migrating from .pyx to .pyxl
searchIndex.description: Pyxle's file extension has changed from `.pyx` to `.pyxl`. This guide explains why and how to update your project.
searchIndex.searchText: Migrating from to Pyxle's file extension has changed from to . This guide explains why and how to update your project. Why the change? The extension is already used by Cython, a wellestablished Python compiler. This collision caused real problems: GitHub misclassification — GitHub's linguist identifies files as Cython, causing repository language stats to show "Cython" instead of "Pyxle." IDE conflicts — VS Code, JetBrains, and other editors default to Cython syntax highlighting when opening fil

searchIndex.title: Runtime API Reference
searchIndex.description: The `pyxle.runtime` module provides decorators and error classes used in `.pyxl` files. These are automatically available in the Python section of every `.pyxl` file -- you do not need to import them
searchIndex.searchText: Runtime API Reference The module provides decorators and error classes used in files. These are automatically available in the Python section of every file you do not need to import them (the compiler injects the import). Marks an async function as a page data loader. Behaviour: Attaches to the function (no wrapping) The function must be (enforced at compile time) Receives a Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/requests/) object Must return a JSONserializable Can return a tuple to set the HTTP

searchIndex.title: Overview — A request, end to end
searchIndex.description: This is the one doc to read first. We're going to follow a single HTTP request all the way from `localhost:8000/` to your browser, naming every component it touches and every transformation it goes th
searchIndex.searchText: Overview — A request, end to end This is the one doc to read first. We're going to follow a single HTTP request all the way from to your browser, naming every component it touches and every transformation it goes through. By the end of this page you'll have a working mental model of the entire framework. To make it concrete, we'll trace the homepage of a fresh project — the one with a Pyxle logo and a "You're ready to build with Pyxle" heading. The starting point: one file Here's the file we're

searchIndex.title: The .pyxl file format
searchIndex.description: A Pyxle page is a single file with two languages in it: Python and JSX. This doc explains *why* that file exists, *what's actually in it*, and
searchIndex.searchText: The file format A Pyxle page is a single file with two languages in it: Python and JSX. This doc explains why that file exists, what's actually in it, and what mental model the rest of the framework uses for it. If you want to know how Pyxle splits the two languages, that's the next doc — The parser. Why one file? Web pages are the unit of work for most web developers. "I'm working on the checkout page. I'm fixing the 404. I'm adding a settings page." The page is the noun. In most frameworks, th
headings: A page with a server loader
What if I'm coming from another framework?

searchIndex.title: The parser
searchIndex.description: The honest answer is: by **parsing**. The Pyxle parser feeds the source to Python's `ast.parse` and uses the structure of the result — and the structure of the failure when parsing fails — to find the
searchIndex.searchText: The parser "How does Pyxle know where the Python ends and the JSX begins?" The honest answer is: by parsing. The Pyxle parser feeds the source to Python's and uses the structure of the result — and the structure of the failure when parsing fails — to find the boundary between the two languages. There are no fence markers, no comment directives, no perline heuristics, no whitespace conventions. There is only a walker, a greedy strategy, and a small handful of carefully chosen guardrails. This doc
headings: Why the obvious approaches don't work
Layer 2: catching broken Python in JSX segments
What this all adds up to

searchIndex.title: The compiler
searchIndex.description: The compiler is the bridge between the parser and the rest of the framework. The parser produces an in-memory `PyxParseResult`; the compiler turns that result into **three files on disk** that the dev
searchIndex.searchText: The compiler The compiler is the bridge between the parser and the rest of the framework. The parser produces an inmemory ; the compiler turns that result into three files on disk that the dev server, the SSR pipeline, and the Vite bundler all consume: This doc walks through what's in each of those files, why they exist as separate artifacts, and the small but important transformations the compiler applies along the way. Files: (70 lines) — toplevel entry (310 lines) — and the runtimeimport inje
headings: Why a custom lexer instead of regex?
The data flow, end to end
Why three files instead of one?

searchIndex.title: Routing
searchIndex.description: Pyxle uses **file-based routing**: the path of a `.pyxl` file inside your `pages/` directory *is* its URL. There is no `@app.route("/")` decorator. There is no router config file. There is no list of
searchIndex.searchText: Routing Pyxle uses filebased routing: the path of a file inside your directory is its URL. There is no decorator. There is no router config file. There is no list of routes maintained by hand. The directory structure is the routing table. This doc explains how that works, exhaustively. By the end you'll know exactly which URL each file maps to, what the dynamic segment syntax means, how layouts compose, and how a request gets dispatched to the right handler. Files: (135 lines) — file path → URL
headings: How a request finds its handler

searchIndex.title: The dev server
searchIndex.description: `pyxle dev` is the command you'll spend the most time with. It runs a Starlette ASGI app on port 8000, a Vite subprocess on port 5173, an SSR worker pool, an incremental compiler, a file watcher, and
searchIndex.searchText: The dev server is the command you'll spend the most time with. It runs a Starlette ASGI app on port 8000, a Vite subprocess on port 5173, an SSR worker pool, an incremental compiler, a file watcher, and a WebSocket error overlay — all coordinated as a single async program. This doc explains how those pieces fit together. By the end you'll understand what every line in the startup banner means, what happens when you save a file, and how to read the dev server's source code. Files (): File What it
headings: When does a layout-only change trigger a page rebuild?
How a request flows through the dev server

searchIndex.title: Server-side rendering
searchIndex.description: Server-side rendering (SSR) is the process of turning a `PageRoute` and an HTTP request into an HTML response. It's the part of the framework that runs on every page load — so it has to be fast, robus
searchIndex.searchText: Serverside rendering Serverside rendering (SSR) is the process of turning a and an HTTP request into an HTML response. It's the part of the framework that runs on every page load — so it has to be fast, robust, and predictable. It's also the part that touches both Python (the loader) and Node.js (the React renderer), so a fair bit of the complexity lives in the bridge between the two languages. This doc walks through the SSR pipeline stage by stage. By the end you'll know exactly what happens be
headings: Stage 1: importing the compiled server module
Why one renderer per request and a pool of workers, not the other way around?

searchIndex.title: Build and serve
searchIndex.description: `pyxle dev` is what you run during development. `pyxle build` and `pyxle serve` are what you run for production. They share most of their code with the dev server but make a few important changes:
searchIndex.searchText: Build and serve is what you run during development. and are what you run for production. They share most of their code with the dev server but make a few important changes: Aspect + Vite dev server on :5173 onetime bundle, then no Vite Source compilation incremental on file change full rebuild, all at once File watcher running not running HMR / React Refresh enabled disabled Module reloading perrequest purge imported once at startup Error responses full stack trace + dev overlay generic Asset se
headings: Why is the SSR worker pool the same in production?

searchIndex.title: The runtime
searchIndex.description: The "runtime" of Pyxle is the **smallest module in the framework**. It contains exactly two decorators (`@server` and `@action`), two exception classes (`LoaderError` and `ActionError`), and nothing e
searchIndex.searchText: The runtime The "runtime" of Pyxle is the smallest module in the framework. It contains exactly two decorators ( and ), two exception classes ( and ), and nothing else. The whole file is 83 lines long including blank lines and docstrings. This is on purpose. The runtime is what your application code imports — it's the contract between your code and the framework. A small contract means a small surface area for bugs, a small mental model for users, and a small attack surface for prompt injection
headings: How the framework actually finds your loader
What if I need *real* request middleware?
What if I want to test my loader?

searchIndex.title: The CLI
searchIndex.description: `pyxle` is the command-line entry point users actually type. It's a
searchIndex.searchText: The CLI is the commandline entry point users actually type. It's a Typer application with five commands: Command Purpose Scaffold a new Pyxle project Run the development server Compile and bundle for production Serve a production build Validate the project without serving This doc explains what each command does, how flags and configuration flow from user input to the rest of the framework, and the design choices behind the CLI surface. Files: (1220 lines) — the Typer commands (115 lines) — scaf
headings: Why does the scaffold ship with the framework?

searchIndex.title: SSR Pipeline
searchIndex.description: This document explains how Pyxle renders pages on the server. Understanding the SSR pipeline is useful for debugging render issues and optimising performance.
searchIndex.searchText: SSR Pipeline This document explains how Pyxle renders pages on the server. Understanding the SSR pipeline is useful for debugging render issues and optimising performance. Overview When a request hits a page route, Pyxle: 1. Resolves the route matches the URL to a file 2. Imports the server module the compiled Python code from the section 3. Runs the loader executes the function with the request 4. Resolves HEAD elements evaluates the variable (static or dynamic) 5. Renders the component runs Re

searchIndex.title: Compiler Internals
searchIndex.description: The Pyxle compiler transforms `.pyxl` files into separate Python and JSX artifacts. This document explains how the compilation process works.
searchIndex.searchText: Compiler Internals The Pyxle compiler transforms files into separate Python and JSX artifacts. This document explains how the compilation process works. Compilation overview When you run or , the compiler: 1. Scans the directory for files 2. Parses each file to separate Python from JSX 3. Writes serverside Python modules to 4. Writes clientside JSX modules to 5. Generates layout composition wrappers in 6. Creates a Vite configuration at The parser The parser () is a statemachine that classifies

searchIndex.title: Frequently Asked Questions
searchIndex.description: Pyxle is a Python-first full-stack web framework. It combines Python server logic with React components in `.pyxl` files, providing file-based routing, server-side rendering, and a development experie
searchIndex.searchText: Frequently Asked Questions General What is Pyxle? Pyxle is a Pythonfirst fullstack web framework. It combines Python server logic with React components in files, providing filebased routing, serverside rendering, and a development experience inspired by Next.js. How is Pyxle different from Next.js? Next.js uses JavaScript/TypeScript for both server and client code. Pyxle uses Python for server logic (data fetching, mutations, API routes) and React for the UI. If your team prefers Python for back
headings: How is Pyxle different from Next.js?
